era_context: The Western Roman Empire is in terminal decline. Romulus Augustulus, a teenager, holds the nominal imperial title in Ravenna while Germanic kingdoms (Ostrogoths, Visigoths, Vandals, Burgundians) control most of the former empire's territory. The Eastern Roman (Byzantine) Empire under Zeno remains strong, controlling the Mediterranean and the Balkans. This is the conventional endpoint of the Western Roman Empire, though the transition was gradual rather than catastrophic.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond the Rhine (sparse archaeological record for this specific year)
Central Asia and the Silk Road (limited contemporary documentation)

events.title: Romulus Augustulus becomes Western Roman Emperor
events.description: Romulus Augustulus, a boy of approximately 14–16 years old, is proclaimed Western Roman Emperor by his father Orestes, a Germanic military strongman who effectively controls Italy. This marks the last recognized emperor of the Western Roman Empire, though his authority is nominal and confined to Italy. The title itself had become meaningless; real power lay with Germanic military commanders and kings. His reign lasted less than a year before deposition.
key_figures: Orestes (father, Germanic military commander and de facto ruler)

sources.name: Jordanes, Getica (6th century compilation of earlier sources)
Procopius, History of the Wars (6th century, Byzantine perspective)

events.certainty_note: The accession is well-attested in multiple near-contemporary and later sources. The exact date (October 475) is given by Marcellinus Comes. However, no contemporary primary sources from Romulus's own reign survive.

events.title: Zeno consolidates power in Eastern Roman Empire after Isaurian civil conflict
events.description: Emperor Zeno, an Isaurian (from the Taurus Mountains in Asia Minor), faces internal opposition from the Eastern Roman aristocracy and military factions who resent Isaurian dominance. By 475, Zeno has largely consolidated his position after years of factional conflict. His reign is marked by efforts to stabilize the empire against Persian threats, manage religious disputes (particularly the Monophysite controversy), and contain Germanic incursions in the Balkans. This year represents a period of relative stability before renewed crises.

sources.name: Procopius, History of the Wars (Byzantine historian, 6th century)

events.certainty_note: Zeno's consolidation of power is well-documented, but the specific events of 475 are not sharply delineated in sources. The broader trajectory is clear; the precise state of affairs in this year is inferred from the general narrative.
cross_references: Part of broader Isaurian dominance in Eastern Roman Empire (470s–480s)
Related to 476-002 (Zeno's response to Odoacer's deposition of Romulus)

events.title: Visigothic Kingdom consolidates control of Iberia and southern Gaul
events.description: The Visigothic Kingdom under King Euric (r. 466–484) continues to expand its territorial control across the Iberian Peninsula and southern Gaul. By 475, the Visigoths have become the dominant power in the western Mediterranean, effectively independent of any nominal Roman authority. Euric's reign is marked by military consolidation, legal codification (the Lex Visigothorum), and the establishment of a stable Germanic kingdom on Roman soil. This represents the successful transformation of a Germanic foederati (allied tribe) into a sovereign state.

sources.name: Isidore of Seville, History of the Goths (7th century, but drawing on earlier sources)
Lex Visigothorum (Visigothic law code, compiled over time, reflects 5th-century practices)

events.certainty_note: Euric's reign and territorial expansion are well-attested. The specific state of Visigothic consolidation in 475 is inferred from the broader narrative of his reign (466–484).
cross_references: Part of broader Germanic settlement and kingdom-formation in the former Western Roman Empire

events.title: Vandal Kingdom controls Mediterranean trade and North Africa
events.description: The Vandal Kingdom under King Gaiseric (r. 428–477) remains at the height of its power, controlling North Africa (including the wealthy grain-producing regions of Numidia and Proconsularis), Sicily, Sardinia, Corsica, and the Balearic Islands. The Vandals maintain a powerful navy that dominates the western Mediterranean, conducting raids and controlling trade routes. This year falls within the period of Vandal prosperity and naval supremacy, before the Byzantine reconquest campaigns of the 530s. The Vandals' control of North Africa represents a major economic and strategic loss for the Eastern Roman Empire.

sources.name: Procopius, History of the Wars (Byzantine perspective on Vandal power)
Victor of Vita, History of the Persecution of the Vandals (5th century, North African perspective)

events.certainty_note: Vandal control of the Mediterranean and North Africa in the 470s is well-documented. Gaiseric's reign and naval dominance are extensively attested.
cross_references: Part of broader Vandal hegemony in the western Mediterranean (428–534)
Contemporary with Eastern Roman efforts to contain Vandal power

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional narrative treats 475 CE as the 'fall of the Western Roman Empire,' with Romulus Augustulus's accession as a symbolic endpoint. However, modern scholarship emphasizes that: (1) The Western Empire had already effectively ceased to exist as a political entity; Romulus's accession changed little on the ground. (2) Germanic kingdoms had been gradually replacing Roman authority for decades; 475 is a conventional marker, not a watershed moment. (3) Roman institutions, law, and culture persisted in Germanic kingdoms; the transition was one of political form, not civilizational collapse. (4) The Eastern Roman Empire remained powerful and considered itself the legitimate continuation of Rome; the 'fall' is a Western historiographic construct. Some scholars argue that dating the Western Empire's end to 476 (Odoacer's deposition of Romulus) is more meaningful than 475, as it marks the end of even nominal imperial succession.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 475 CE is moderate but heavily skewed toward the Mediterranean and Eastern Roman perspectives. Most surviving sources are Byzantine (Procopius, Marcellinus Comes) or later compilations (Jordanes, Isidore of Seville). Contemporary sources from the Western Empire are almost entirely absent; we know little of daily life, administration, or events outside the imperial court. Germanic kingdoms' internal affairs are poorly documented. Sub-Saharan Africa, northern Europe, and Asia are virtually absent from the record. The sources are also shaped by Byzantine and later Christian historiography, which emphasizes political succession and religious disputes over economic or social change. Archaeological evidence (coins, pottery, settlement patterns) provides some corrective, but is not systematically integrated into the narrative for this specific year.

graph_edges.note: Romulus Augustulus's accession led directly to his deposition by Odoacer in 476, marking the conventional end of the Western Roman Empire.
Romulus's accession followed the deposition of Julius Nepos by Orestes in 474.
Zeno's consolidation of power in the East enabled his response to Odoacer's actions in the West.
Visigothic consolidation under Euric set the stage for the kingdom's dominance in Iberia until the Frankish invasion of 507.
Vandal naval dominance in 475 would be challenged by Byzantine reconquest campaigns beginning in 533 under Justinian.
